plan.description: The objective of this project is to develop a publicly accessible infrastructure that enables easy access and searchability of blockchain data in accordance with the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Replicable) of open science. This infrastructure aims to promote complete transparency and reproducibility of scientific analysis results in the blockchain field, thereby facilitating the growth and collaboration of new and existing applications.
plan.title: FairOnChain

reference.label: Haute Ecole de Gestion de Geneve HES-SO

description.description:
The code developed within the FairOnChain project is essential for enabling the extraction, processing, analysis, and sharing of blockchain data. This includes scripts for raw data extraction from blockchain nodes, transformation into structured formats, and the creation of secondary datasets such as labeled or aggregated data.

The codebase also powers key functionalities, including:

    Data access APIs for both primary and secondary datasets.
    Automation pipelines for metadata generation and dataset versioning.
    Integration with decentralized storage systems (e.g., IPFS) for efficient dataset distribution.

The primary purpose of this code is to ensure the reproducibility of workflows, scalability of data management processes, and alignment with FAIR principles. It will be made open-source to encourage reuse, collaboration, and innovation in the blockchain research community.
description.label: FairOnChain code

descriptionTemplate.label: CHIST-ERA Software Management
descriptionTemplate.description: This template is adapted from the ELIXIR research community.

options.label: Publicly available (e.g. GitHub, via URL, etc.)
options.value: Publicly available (e.g. GitHub, via URL, etc.)

labelData.label: Please specify why this is not relevant / specified to you

fieldSet.title: How can the software be accessed by third parties?
Software without a license cannot be used by others. Does your software have a license?
fieldSet.description: You may use the License Clearance Tool to check the license compatibility possibilities across different libraries and repositories used to develop your software.

labelData.label: Please add other license(s), if applicable

fieldSet.title: If yes, what is the license of your software?
fieldSet.description: You may also check the SPDX List to understand what each license covers .

visibilityRule.value: Other (for more than one, please add multiple rows)

labelData.label: Please add other documentation URL. For more than one, please add multiple rows.

fieldSet.title: What type of documentation is available, provided with the software and delivered under the same conditions?
Is the purpose of the software stated in the documentation?
Does the documentation describe how to test the software?
Does the documentation describe how to use the software?
Does the documentation describe how to build the software?
Does the documentation describe how to deploy the software?
Does the documentation describe how to install the software?

labelData.label: Provide a link to where the testing is

fieldSet.title: What type of testing do you use?
Are sample data and/or parameters that can be used to test the software available with the source code?
Do you use existing and standard input/output formats?

selectData.label: What version control system do you use?

fieldSet.title: Do you use a version control system?
Do you use Semantic Versioning?
fieldSet.description: For more information go here: https://semver.org/.

fieldSet.title: Do you provide releases of your software?
How do you define language-specific dependencies of your software and their version?
fieldSet.description: For instance Maven (for Java), requirements.txt or environment.yml (Python), package.json (JS)

fieldSet.title: How do you capture the environment (operating system, system-level libraries) necessary to run the software (e.g. containers, conda, virtual machine)?

labelData.label: If yes, where can they be found? Please provide the URL, if available.

fieldSet.title: Do you provide input and output examples that can be used to reproduce the functioning of your software?
Do you state how to report bugs and/or usability problems by the software user(s)?
fieldSet.description: This could be a simple sentence in the software documentation, stating an email, or an issue tracker, or a direct messaging protocol, or even a statement that bug reporting/issues are not supported.

fieldSet.title: Do you include citation information (i.e. how to cite your software in the form of citation.cff, codemeta.json or bibtex)?
Do the releases have a persistent global unique identifier (such as release on Zenodo with DOI, snapshot or/and release referenced on Software Heritage with SWHID)?
fieldSet.description: For more information on PIDs and the differences between the various types (intrinsic, extrinsic, etc), please see here.

field.textValue: Yes

Containerization:

    Docker: Encapsulates the operating system, system-level libraries, and dependencies in a portable and reproducible container defined by a Dockerfile.

Virtual Environments:

    Conda: Captures Python dependencies and system-level libraries in an environment.yml file.
    Virtualenv: Isolates Python dependencies in lightweight setups.

Environment Variables:

    .env files define environment-specific variables (e.g., API keys, database URLs).

Documentation:

    Detailed setup instructions list supported operating systems, system-level libraries, and configuration steps.
http://www.faironchain.org/documentation

textListValues.textListValue: Publicly available (e.g. GitHub, via URL, etc.)

field.textValue: Yes

Sample Data:

    Primary Blockchain Data:
    A small, representative subset of raw blockchain data (e.g., blocks, transactions, and smart contract interactions) will be included for testing purposes. This data is anonymized and does not contain sensitive or proprietary information.
    Secondary Data:
    Processed datasets in SQL and CSV formats, such as aggregated statistics or labeled blockchain addresses, will be included to test data transformation and analysis workflows.

Parameters and Configuration Files:

    Default configuration files will be provided to guide users in setting up the software. These include:
        API keys and endpoints for interacting with blockchain nodes.
        Parameters for data cleaning, aggregation, and filtering.
    Example queries and commands for interacting with APIs or SQL datasets.

Integration with IPFS:

    CIDs for small, preloaded datasets hosted on IPFS will be shared to test the software's capability to retrieve and manage data from decentralized storage.

Automated Tests:

    Unit tests and sample datasets will be included to validate each module of the software (e.g., data extraction, processing, and API functionality).

Documentation:

    Detailed user guides and examples will accompany the codebase to help users understand how to utilize the sample data and parameters for testing.
Yes
No
No

Dependency Management Tools:

    Python: requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, or Pipfile for listing and locking dependencies.
    JavaScript/Node.js: package.json and package-lock.json for specifying and locking dependencies.

Versioning Practices:

    Pinned Versions: Critical dependencies are locked to specific versions for stability.
    Version Ranges: Non-critical libraries may allow flexibility in updates.

Documentation and Setup:

    Dependencies are documented in README.md or equivalent with installation instructions.
    Files like .env or Dockerfile ensure consistent environment setup.

Automated Tools:

    Dependency audits and CI/CD pipelines are used for compatibility testing and updates.
Yes

description.description: This description outlines the data management activities related to the collection, structuring, and sharing of blockchain transaction data from major networks (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum). The goal is to ensure compliance with FAIR principles by providing standardized APIs and open datasets for research and societal use. Key activities include data curation, metadata enrichment, and access control to balance openness with resource constraints.
description.label: Blockchain data

descriptionTemplate.label: CHIST-ERA Data Management
descriptionTemplate.description: This is a DMP template created based on the CHIST-ERA RDM policy and DMP template.

page.title: Data Description And Collection Or Reuse of Existing Data

options.label: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)
options.value: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)

options.label: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
options.value: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)

options.label: Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)
options.value: Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)

fieldSet.title: Give details on the kind of data
fieldSet.description:
The main distinction between data types is between primary data and secondary data. Data collection may contain both primary and secondary data depending on the source where they have been derived from. 

  • primary data is data that have been collected for the first time and have not undergone through data processing and/or analysis, yet. 

  • secondary data is data that have been cleaned up, analysed and shared by others (published or unpublished) and they are those that are being typically reused. 

In addition, types of data unveil how they have been collected so they also depend on the process that led to their generation.

fieldSet.title: Give details on the data format
fieldSet.description:
Data may be found in different formats. Usually, outputs of specific instruments/ services/ tools where data are generated and/ or processed have a default way of exposing their outputs following specific standards. 

Most common distinctions between formats are proprietary and non proprietary formats, because they may impact data access in the long-term.

options.label: de facto standard in my discipline
options.value: de facto standard in my discipline

options.label: de iure standard in my discipline
options.value: de iure standard in my discipline

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Justify the use of certain formats
fieldSet.description: For example, decisions may be based on staff expertise within the host organisation, a preference for open formats, standards accepted by data repositories, widespread usage within the research community, or on the software or equipment that will be used.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Give preference to open and standard formats as they facilitate sharing and long-term re-use of data (several repositories provide lists of such ‘preferred formats’).

fieldSet.title: Give details on the volumes
fieldSet.description: Give measurable examples per type of output based on common practices in the field. These might be relevant to the volumes of data and how many bytes of storage they occupy, numbers of objects, files, rows, and columns.

section.title: What data (for example the kind, formats, and volumes), will be collected or produced?

referenceTypeData.label: Select the tools used for this activity

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Explain which methodologies or software will be used if new data are collected or produced or if third party data are used
Explain how data provenance will be documented

section.title: How will new data be collected or produced?

fieldSet.title: Indicate which metadata will be provided to help others identify and discover the data
fieldSet.description:
Metadata is data about data and is an essential set of information describing scientific outputs, in the form of either physical or digital objects, in a machine-readable format. According to the expected use, metadata can be given different attributes. Most common type which enables discovery and identification are descriptive metadata. descriptive metadata contain information about key aspects needed to search for and successfully find a given scientific output, e.g. by its: 

  • title, 

  • author/creator, 

  • abstract, 

  • keywords

Moreover, metadata may be used for describing a service or a scientific instrument.

fieldSet.title: Indicate which metadata standards will be used
fieldSet.description: Use community metadata standards where these are in place.

fieldSet.title: Indicate how the data will be organised during the project
fieldSet.description: Νaming conventions should be clearly described in order to make data understandable by other researchers that may reuse them after the project ends. Μentioning for example conventions, version control, and folder structures. consistent, well-ordered research data will be easier to find, understand, and re-use.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Check out some examples of file naming best practices: http://tiny.cc/op8lcz

labelData.label: Please provide information and links to the documentation, should it is available

fieldSet.title: Consider what other documentation is needed to enable re-use
fieldSet.description: This may include information on the methodology used to collect the data, analytical and procedural information, definitions of variables, units of measurement, and so on.

section.description: For example the methodology of data collection and way of organizing data
section.title: What metadata and documentation will accompany the data?

options.label: To compare and combine with other data
options.value: To compare and combine with other data

options.label: To follow-up research on a specific area
options.value: To follow-up research on a specific area

options.label: To contribute to the wider community (researchers, public authorities, citizen scientists)
options.value: To contribute to the wider community (researchers, public authorities, citizen scientists)

labelData.label: Please provide URL/Location of the DMP where reused data are documented

fieldSet.title: How will existing data be re-used?
fieldSet.description: Apart from data produced within your research, you might want to repurpose data that have been produced and shared by others, in different research context. Ιf you are planning to use other researchers’ data in your research there are a couple of things that you need to take into consideration, such as copyright of datasets, permissions provided through licenses, ethical aspects for reuse, etc.

fieldSet.title: Where can re-used data be found?
fieldSet.description: Select the repository/ies where data are hosted.

fieldSet.title: Which data will be re-used?
fieldSet.description: Select the datasets that will be reused. Make sure that you select the correct filenames and versions!

page.title: Storage And Backup During The Research Process

section.title: Storage And Backup During The Research Process

fieldSet.title: Explain how the data will be recovered in the event of an incident and describe the main risks and how these will be managed
fieldSet.description: Consider data protection, particularly if your data is sensitive for example containing personal data, politically sensitive information, or trade secrets.

section.title: How will data security and protection of sensitive data be taken care of during the research?

page.title: Legal And Ethical Requirements, Codes Of Conduct

section.title: Legal And Ethical Requirements, Codes Of Conduct

labelData.label: How will compliance with legislation on personal data and on security be ensured?

fieldSet.title: Are there any personal data to be formulated?
fieldSet.description: It is possible that data are sensitive, such as personal or confidential, and hence they can not be shared directly to others. For those types of data, specific processes need to be made in order to ensure their reliable exchange in the short and long-term.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find out more about sensitive data and how to process them: https://www.openaire.eu/sensitive-data-guide

fieldSet.title: Explain whether there is a managed access procedure in place for authorised users of personal data

section.description: Ensure that when dealing with personal data data protection laws (for example GDPR) are complied with: ›› Gain informed consent for preservation and/or sharing of personal data. ›› Consider anonymisation of personal data for preservation and/or sharing (truly anonymous data are no longer considered personal data). ›› Consider pseudonymisation of personal data (the main difference with anonymisation is that pseudonymisation is reversible). ›› Consider encryption which is seen as a special case of pseudonymisation (the encryption key must be stored separately from the data, for instance by a trusted third party).
section.title: Personal data
How will other legal issues, such as intellectual property rights and ownership, be managed? What legislation is applicable?

fieldSet.title: Who will be the owner(s) of the data?
fieldSet.description: Meaning who will have the rights to control access.

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer
Please give more information, including links to specific documents (if available)

fieldSet.title: Explain what access or restrictions will apply to the data?

section.description: Make sure to cover these matters of rights to control access to data for multi-partner projects and multiple data owners, in the consortium agreement.
section.title: Data ownership and accessibility

fieldSet.title: Explain which intellectual property and how will they be dealt with
Are there any restrictions on the re-use of third-party data?

options.label: Legal issues such as IP rights
options.value: Legal issues such as IP rights

options.label: Environmental issues such as the locating of endangered species
options.value: Environmental issues such as the locating of endangered species

options.label: Security issues for organisations or countries
options.value: Security issues for organisations or countries

fieldSet.title: What ethical issues and codes of conduct are there, and how will they be taken into account?

section.description:
Consider whether ethical issues can affect how data are stored and transferred, who can see or use them, and how long they are kept. Demonstrate awareness of these aspects and respective planning. 
Follow the national and international codes of conducts and institutional ethical guidelines, and check if ethical review (for example by an ethics committee) is required for data collection in the research project.
section.title: Ethical issues

options.label: Deposit in a FAIR-enabling data repository
options.value: Deposit in a trustworthy data repository

options.label: Use of a secure data service
options.value: Use of a secure data service

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Explain how the data will be discoverable and shared
fieldSet.description: Please note that “shared” refers to sharing with people outside the Project consortium.

fieldSet.title: Outline the plan for data preservation and give information on how long the data will be retained
Explain when the data will be made available
Indicate the expected timely release
Will exclusive use of the data be claimed?
Indicate whether and for how long data sharing will be embargoed or restricted
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For example to publish, protect intellectual property, or seek patents.

fieldSet.title: Indicate how the data will be used
Is it necessary to restrict access to certain communities or to apply a data sharing agreement?
Explain how and why and what action will be taken to overcome or to minimise restrictions

section.description: Please check with funders' or institutions's policies which might apply an embargo on data access.
section.title: How and when will data be shared? Are there possible restrictions to data sharing or embargo reasons?

fieldSet.title: Indicate what data must be retained or destroyed for contractual, legal, or regulatory purposes
Indicate how it will be decided what data to keep
fieldSet.description: Decision trees for digital preservation: https://www.canada.ca/en/heritage-information-network/services/digital-preservation/decision-tree.html

options.label: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)
options.value: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)

options.label: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
options.value: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)

options.label: Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)
options.value: Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)

fieldSet.title: Describe the data to be preserved long-term
Indicate where the data will be deposited
fieldSet.description: Select a repository that will host the research data outputs for long-term preservation.

fieldSet.title: Indicate how the data will be shared
Indicate whether potential users need specific tools to access and (re-)use the data.

section.description: For example a data repository or archive.
section.title: How will data for preservation be selected, and where data will be preserved long-term?

fieldSet.title: What type of persistent identifier (PID) will be used?
fieldSet.description:
Persistent identifiers should be applied so that data can be reliably and efficiently located and referred to. Persistent identifiers also help to track citations and re-use. A persistent identifier or PID is a long-lasting reference to a resource. Whatever resource it refers to, the primary purpose of the PID is to provide the information required to reliably identify, verify and locate it. A PID may be connected to a set of metadata describing an item rather than to the item itself.
Typically, a trustworthy, long-term repository will provide a persistent identifier.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Learn more about PIDs and how to use them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-can-identifiers-improve-the-dissemination-of-your-research-outputs

section.title: How will the application of a unique and persistent identifier to each data set be ensured?

fieldSet.title: Outline the roles and responsibilities for data management/stewardship activities
fieldSet.description: Name responsible individual(s) where possible.
fieldSet.extendedDescription:
For example:
  • data capture, 
  • metadata production, 
  • data quality, 
  • storage and backup, 
  • data archiving, and 
  • data sharing.

section.description: Provide names and responsibilities of researchers’ or data managers’ data management and stewardship activities that are performed throughout the project. Follow the research data management lifecycle to guide your steps in data capture, metadata production, data quality, storage and backup, data archiving, and data sharing.
section.title: Who will be responsible for data management?

fieldSet.title: Explain how the necessary resources to prepare the data for sharing/preservation have been costed in
fieldSet.description: Research Data Management is not a costless procedure, so it is important that it is thought through at the planning stage of research. Carefully consider and justify any resources needed to deliver the data. These may include storage costs, hardware, staff time, costs of preparing data for deposit, and repository charges.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To understand what costs are allocated across the steps of a RDM lifecycle, how to estimate them and how to prevent them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-comply-to-h2020-mandates-rdm-costs

fieldSet.title: Indicate whether additional resources will be needed to prepare data for deposit or to meet any charges from data repositories

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Explain how much cost provisionally is needed
fieldSet.description: You can use: Data Management Costing Tool (zingtree.com)

section.title: What resources will be dedicated to data management and ensuring that data will be FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Re-usable)?

reference.label: Haute Ecole de Gestion de Geneve HES-SO

field.textValue:
Recovery of Primary Data:

    Primary data can be re-extracted from the blockchain nodes operated by the project. This ensures the data remains available without relying on large-scale backups.

Recovery of Secondary Data:

    Secondary data can be recalculated from primary data using well-documented scripts and pipelines, ensuring full reproducibility.

Recovery of Critical Data:

    Critical files (scripts and documentation) will be restored from version-controlled repositories with regular backups stored in redundant systems (e.g., cloud or local storage).

Risk Management:

    Hardware Failure: Managed through RAID 5 configurations to provide fault tolerance.
    Loss or Corruption of Primary/Secondary Data: Mitigated by the ability to reconstruct data from blockchain nodes and processing pipelines.
    Loss of Documentation or Code: Restored from versioned backups and redundant storage.
    IPFS Availability Issues: Handled by the inherent redundancy of the IPFS network, supported by users and the consortium.

Incident Response:

    Primary and secondary data will be reconstructed if necessary.
    Critical files will be recovered using version-controlled backups and external duplication.

1. Institutional Infrastructure

    Utilization of Institutional Resources:
        Partnering institutions will provide access to their existing infrastructure for hosting blockchain nodes, IPFS gateways, and SQL servers.
        This minimizes initial setup costs and leverages institutional expertise in data management.

    Institutional Data Repositories:
        Metadata and supplementary datasets will be deposited in institutional repositories like Zenodo or similar platforms, often free of charge for research outputs.

2. Consortium Collaboration

    Shared Infrastructure Among Consortium Members:
        Consortium members will contribute resources, such as IPFS nodes, to ensure decentralized hosting of large datasets.
        This collaborative approach distributes costs and responsibilities while ensuring high availability and fault tolerance.

    Governance Contributions:
        Consortium members will contribute funding for maintenance and operational costs, as outlined in the governance structure.

3. Blockchain Ecosystem Grants

    Layer 1 and Layer 2 Blockchain Grants:
        The project seeks financial support from blockchain foundations (e.g., Ethereum Foundation, Polygon, Avalanche) that provide grants for research and infrastructure development.
        Such grants align with the project’s goal of enabling transparent and FAIR data sharing for the blockchain ecosystem.

    Community Funding:
        Proposals for funding will also be submitted to decentralized autonomous organizations (DAOs) within the blockchain ecosystem that support open data initiatives.

4. Research and Innovation Funding

    Grants from Research Funding Bodies:
        Funding applications have been or will be submitted to major research funding organizations, such as the EU Horizon Europe, CHIST-ERA, or national funding bodies, for infrastructure development and FAIR data sharing.
        These grants support the alignment with open science principles and global data preservation standards.

    Infrastructure as a Lever for Further Research Funding:
        The project’s infrastructure will enable additional research initiatives, serving as a basis for future grant proposals.

5. Commercial Revenue Streams

    Usage Fees for Commercial Access:
        Commercial users of the FairOnChain infrastructure (e.g., APIs, secondary datasets, and advanced analytics) will be charged usage fees.
        Revenue will be reinvested into maintaining and expanding the data infrastructure.

    Tiered Access Models:
        Free or subsidized access will be provided for academic and non-profit users, while premium features (e.g., enhanced APIs, priority access) will generate revenue from commercial entities.

6. Sustainability via Cost Sharing

    Distributed Costs for Shared Resources:
        Costs for running blockchain nodes, hosting IPFS gateways, and managing SQL databases will be shared across consortium members and institutional partners.
        The use of decentralized storage (IPFS) reduces reliance on expensive centralized hosting solutions.

7. Long-Term Sustainability

    Reinvestment of Revenue:
        Revenue generated through commercial services and grants will be reinvested into maintaining and scaling the infrastructure.

    Periodic Funding Applications:
        Regular applications to blockchain ecosystem grants and research funding bodies will ensure ongoing support.

The data generated and processed in the FairOnChain project will be provided in open and widely used formats to ensure accessibility and long-term usability.

    Primary Data:
        Primarily stored in CSV format for simplicity and broad compatibility.
        JSON may be used for hierarchical or more complex data structures when necessary.

    Secondary Data:
        Primarily stored in a relational SQL database to facilitate structured queries and relationships between datasets.
        CSV will also be used for sharing smaller or simpler datasets.
        Parquet may be used for larger datasets or when more efficient storage and processing are required.

These formats and storage strategies are selected to balance usability, efficiency, and flexibility across a range of use cases, ensuring compatibility with both research and operational requirements.

1. Primary Data (Blockchain Data):

    Access Requirements:
        Data will be accessible through API endpoints that expose raw blockchain data for specific use cases (e.g., transactions, blocks, smart contracts).
        Alternatively, users can retrieve data via IPFS clients using the provided Content Identifiers (CIDs).
    Re-use Requirements:
        API access requires tools or libraries capable of making HTTP requests (e.g., curl, Postman, or programming languages like Python, using libraries such as requests or web3.py).
        Users should have a basic understanding of blockchain structures and APIs to integrate the data into their workflows.

2. Secondary Data (Processed and Aggregated Datasets):

    Access Requirements:
        Users can interact with the secondary datasets via:
            APIs: Designed for direct querying of structured datasets (e.g., aggregated statistics, labeled address data).
            SQL databases: For advanced queries using SQL clients (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL).
            IPFS clients: For accessing large dynamic datasets.
    Re-use Requirements:
        API clients or web-based tools to query and retrieve specific subsets of secondary data.
        Knowledge of SQL and analysis tools (e.g., Python with pandas or R with dplyr) is recommended for complex queries or advanced analyses.

3. Dynamic IPFS Datasets:

    Access Requirements:
        Users will require an IPFS client or an interface integrated with IPFS to fetch datasets directly.
        APIs may also expose IPFS-referenced datasets, allowing easier retrieval without installing an IPFS client.
    Re-use Requirements:
        Standard data manipulation and analysis tools (e.g., Python, R, or Excel) depending on the format of the data (e.g., JSON, CSV).

4. API Integration:

    Features:
        APIs will expose both real-time and historical data from primary and secondary sources.
        Documentation will include examples of API usage, endpoints, authentication, and query parameters.
    Re-use Requirements:
        Tools such as Postman or programming languages (e.g., Python, JavaScript) will be used to interact with the APIs.
        Advanced users can integrate APIs into their workflows or software for automation and further analysis.

The following repositories and mechanisms will be used for data deposition:

    Zenodo for Metadata:
        Role: Zenodo will store the metadata for all datasets, including descriptions, licensing terms, and links to primary and secondary datasets.
        Why: Zenodo is FAIR-compliant and widely used for academic data sharing.

    IPFS for Data Storage:
        Role: The primary and secondary datasets will be distributed and preserved via IPFS (InterPlanetary File System). This ensures decentralized, scalable, and long-term access to large datasets.
        Why: IPFS is ideal for managing large, dynamic datasets like blockchain data and derived outputs.

    SQL Databases:
        Role: Secondary datasets, particularly SQL databases, will be maintained in queryable environments and archived periodically for distribution.
        Why: SQL databases support efficient querying and analysis for researchers and developers.
Repository
Primary data

textListValues.textListValue: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)

field.textValue: Secondary data

textListValues.textListValue: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)
Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)

field.textValue:
To reproduce and validate findings
Existing labeled data, sourced from established datasets or third-party providers (e.g., blockchain analytics platforms, community-curated datasets), will be reused to validate prior research and ensure the reproducibility of blockchain analytics findings.

To compare and combine with other data
Labeled datasets, including protocol names or account classifications, will be integrated with raw blockchain transaction data collected directly from full nodes (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum). This allows for advanced comparative analysis of protocol-specific activity or account behaviors.

To follow-up research on a specific area
Researchers can leverage labeled datasets to explore specific topics, such as token flow tracking, DeFi protocol interactions, or behavior patterns associated with labeled account types (e.g., exchanges, smart contract wallets). These labeled data will primarily originate from third-party providers or collaborative research contributions.

To contribute to the wider community (researchers, public authorities, citizen scientists)
By sharing curated and labeled datasets, including their provenance (e.g., community contributions or publicly available labeled datasets), the project aims to support transparency and foster deeper analysis of blockchain ecosystems.

The provenance of all data in the FairOnChain project will be documented to ensure transparency, traceability, and reproducibility. This will include both primary, secondary and third party data, with the following measures in place:

Primary Data:
        Source Documentation:
        Each dataset will include metadata specifying the blockchain network (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum) and the specific node or API used for data collection.
            Example metadata fields:
                Blockchain name.
                Node software/version (e.g., Bitcoin Core v23.0).
                Block range or timestamp of data extraction.
        Data Collection Logs:
        Logs of data extraction processes, including timestamps, scripts used, and any errors encountered, will be maintained for audit purposes.

Secondary Data:
        Transformation Records:
        All processing steps applied to raw data (e.g., cleaning, aggregation, filtering) will be documented in detail. This will include the methodology, software versions, and specific scripts or workflows used.
            Example: “Transaction aggregation script (Python, version 3.9) executed on dataset XYZ on 2024-01-15.”
        Metadata Standards:
        Metadata will be enriched using standards such as Dublin Core or schema.org, capturing key information about data origins, processing steps, and creators.

Provenance Metadata Repository:
        A centralized metadata repository will store all provenance information, linked to corresponding datasets.
        Tools such as Git (for script version control) and data catalogs (e.g., CKAN) will ensure accessibility and version tracking of provenance records.

Provenance for Third-Party Data:
        For external data sources, details such as the provider (e.g., CoinGecko API), retrieval date, and any transformations applied will be documented.
        Example: “Price data retrieved via CoinGecko API on 2024-02-01, filtered for top 100 cryptocurrencies by market cap.”

Primary Data:
        Use Case:
            Primary onchain data will serve as the foundational dataset for all analyses, research, and derivative works.
            Researchers and developers will use this data to explore blockchain activity, transaction flows, smart contract interactions, and tokenomics.
        Audience:
            Academic researchers, non-profit organizations, and commercial entities analyzing blockchain ecosystems.

    Secondary Data:
        Use Case:
            Secondary datasets, such as curated SQL datasets or labeled datasets distributed via IPFS, will enable:
                In-depth Analysis: Investigating specific blockchain use cases like DeFi, NFTs, and transaction clustering.
                Model Training: Building machine learning models using labeled datasets for address classification or anomaly detection.
                Cross-Chain Comparisons: Comparing blockchain ecosystems based on aggregated metrics.
        Audience:
            Secondary datasets are targeted at a broader audience, including data scientists, blockchain developers, and policy analysts.

    Critical Data (Code and Documentation):
        Use Case:
            Code and documentation will ensure the reproducibility of research and the creation of new tools for data analysis.
            Open-source developers will use this information to build upon the project’s work, while consortium members may extend the pipelines for future studies.
        Audience:
            Developers, consortium members, and independent researchers working on blockchain infrastructure and analysis.

Purpose of Use:

    Research:
        Enabling open and reproducible blockchain research aligned with the FAIR principles.
        Supporting academic publications, theses, and institutional studies.

    Development:
        Providing datasets for building blockchain analytics platforms and decision-support systems.

    Policy and Advocacy:
        Helping policymakers and NGOs understand blockchain ecosystems and their societal impact.

    Commercial Applications:
        Commercial users will leverage the data for product development, business intelligence, and market analysis, while adhering to licensing terms and usage restrictions.

Primary Data:

    Reason:
    Blockchain data is inherently decentralized and preserved across blockchain nodes. No destruction is required, and the data is retained indefinitely as part of the blockchain ecosystem.
        Legal Basis: The decentralized nature of public blockchains ensures compliance with open access and public availability principles.

Secondary Data:

    Reason:
        Secondary datasets (e.g., labeled datasets or aggregated SQL data) are retained for reproducibility and long-term research purposes.
        These datasets will be retained for at least 10 years to comply with research best practices and ensure accessibility for validation and further analysis.
        Contractual Basis: Retention of secondary data aligns with licensing terms (e.g., CC BY 4.0) and obligations to provide access to academic and non-profit communities.

Critical Data (Code and Documentation):

    Reason:
    Code and documentation are critical for ensuring the reproducibility of research outcomes and for future updates.
        Contractual Basis: Open-source licenses (e.g., MIT, GPL) require code availability for derivative works and ongoing use.
Retained

Access to the data will be mediated to ensure sustainability of the infrastructure while prioritizing academic research and balancing resource demands. The access framework includes:

    Primary Data:
        Access Mode: Publicly distributed via IPFS to minimize centralized storage and bandwidth costs.
        Restrictions: None, as primary data is publicly sourced from blockchain nodes. However, excessive resource usage by individual users (e.g., large-scale downloads) will be monitored to maintain system stability.

    Secondary Data (Derived or Curated Datasets):
        Access Mode: Mediated access based on user roles to ensure fair use of resources:
            Academic Users (AU): Researchers are prioritized with free access to non-commercial resources. This encourages open science while minimizing barriers for academic studies.
            Non-Profit Users (NPU): Access is granted for non-commercial purposes, ensuring their needs are met while balancing system costs.
            Commercial Users (CU): Paid access is required to offset the infrastructure costs, with tiered quotas for API usage, bulk downloads, or database queries.
        Restrictions: Licensing terms limit redistribution of secondary datasets and enforce fair resource allocation.

    Critical Data (Code and Documentation):
        Access Mode: Restricted to consortium members and authorized contributors to safeguard intellectual property and ensure integrity of the project’s reproducibility assets.
        Restrictions: Access to these resources is tightly controlled to prevent misuse and ensure their long-term preservation.

    Rationale for Mediated Access:
        High Infrastructure Costs: The cost of maintaining the blockchain nodes, IPFS distribution, and data processing infrastructure is significant. Commercial licensing and tiered access for non-academic users help recover operational expenses.
        Prioritization of Researchers: Academic users are prioritized to foster open scientific research while ensuring infrastructure sustainability. A registration process ensures that researchers can access resources efficiently without overloading the system.
        Demand-Based Resource Allocation: Access quotas are adjusted dynamically based on user demand and infrastructure load to maintain system performance and availability.

    Licensing and Oversight:
        Non-commercial access is provided under a Creative Commons BY-NC-SA license.
        Commercial use requires paid licensing agreements, managed and enforced by the Steering Committee (SC).

    Mediation Process:
        Users must register and specify their intended use (academic, non-profit, or commercial).
        Automated systems manage access requests based on user roles, quotas, and system load. Critical decisions are escalated to the SC when necessary.

Data Capture
Responsible for extracting raw data from blockchain nodes and third-party sources.
Metadata Production
Creates and maintains metadata for datasets to ensure compliance with FAIR principles.
Data Quality
Validates the accuracy, consistency, and reliability of both primary and secondary datasets.
Storage and Backup
Ensures secure storage and redundancy for all datasets and critical documentation.
Data Archiving
Manages the long-term preservation and periodic archiving of datasets and metadata.
Data Sharing
Facilitates data access and sharing, including API management and metadata linking.
Governance Oversight
Ensures alignment of data management practices with project goals, legal requirements, and ethical standards.

Data Capture:
        Role: Responsible for setting up and managing data extraction processes from blockchain nodes and third-party data sources.
        Responsibilities:
            Maintain and monitor blockchain nodes for data extraction.
            Ensure the completeness and integrity of raw data.
        Responsible Individual(s): [S. Augusto]

    Metadata Production:
        Role: Responsible for creating and maintaining metadata for all datasets to ensure compliance with FAIR principles.
        Responsibilities:
            Generate metadata entries for datasets stored in Zenodo or other repositories.
            Include licensing information, dataset descriptions, and technical documentation.
        Responsible Individual(s): [S. Augusto]

    Data Quality:
        Role: Responsible for validating the accuracy, consistency, and reliability of datasets (primary and secondary).
        Responsibilities:
            Implement validation scripts to verify data integrity and correctness.
            Conduct periodic reviews to identify and address errors or inconsistencies in the data.
        Responsible Individual(s): [C. Lebrun]

    Storage and Backup:
        Role: Responsible for ensuring secure storage and redundancy of critical datasets and documentation.
        Responsibilities:
            Set up IPFS storage for primary and secondary datasets.
            Manage RAID-5 disk storage for SQL-based datasets.
            Implement regular backups for critical project files (e.g., code, documentation).
        Responsible Individual(s): [V.Berisha]

    Data Archiving:
        Role: Responsible for archiving datasets, including ensuring compliance with retention policies and data lifecycle management.
        Responsibilities:
            Periodically archive secondary datasets and metadata in Zenodo or similar repositories.
            Monitor dataset usage to determine whether to continue archiving or deprecate specific datasets.
        Responsible Individual(s): [S. Augusto]

    Data Sharing:
        Role: Responsible for facilitating data access and sharing with researchers, non-profits, and commercial users.
        Responsibilities:
            Manage API access to datasets, including user authentication and quota allocation.
            Oversee the sharing of IPFS CIDs and ensure metadata links are up-to-date.
        Responsible Individual(s): [V. Berisha]

Governance Oversight

    Role: The Steering Committee oversees all data management activities to ensure alignment with project goals, legal requirements, and ethical considerations.
    Responsibilities:
        Review and approve data management policies.
        Address conflicts or challenges related to data use or sharing.
    Responsible Individual(s): [A. Gaudinat]

textListValues.textListValue: To compare and combine with other data
To follow-up research on a specific area
To contribute to the wider community (researchers, public authorities, citizen scientists)

field.textValue:
Primary Data Collection:

Methodology:
    Full blockchain nodes will be deployed for each target blockchain network. These nodes will collect raw transaction data directly from the network, ensuring the data's accuracy and completeness.
    Software and Tools:
  • full nodes software
  • ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines to extract (JSON-RPC), process and store raw data efficiently.

Secondary Data Production:

    Methodology:
    Data cleaning, aggregation, and enrichment processes will be applied to raw transaction data to create curated datasets. Aggregated views may include behavioral profiles of accounts, NFT analytics, or transaction graphs.
    Software and Tools:
  • Python for data processing and enrichment.
  • SQL-based databases for storing structured datasets.
  • Parquet for efficient storage of large datasets.
  • Visualization and custom web-based dashboards for secondary data exploration.
Third-Party Data Integration:

    Methodology:
    Relevant third-party data sources (e.g., price feeds, Labels of smartcontracts) will be integrated into the datasets to provide contextual insights.
    Software and Tools:
  • APIs (e.g., CoinGecko, Dune Analytics) for retrieving external data.
  •  Data merging and transformation using Python (e.g., pandas library).

Deposit in a FAIR-enabling data repository

    - Relevance: Metadata and descriptions of secondary datasets will be deposited in FAIR-enabling repositories like Zenodo, while the datasets themselves (due to their size and dynamic nature) will primarily be distributed through SQL databases or dynamic IPFS datasets for some use cases.
    - Example: Zenodo will store metadata, including dataset descriptions, SQL schemas, licensing terms, and IPFS CIDs (for datasets made available on IPFS).

Use of Another Mechanism

    - Relevance: Secondary datasets, particularly those in SQL format, will be accessible through direct SQL queries or as exports provided via mediated access. For dynamic datasets, IPFS will be used to share the data in a decentralized and scalable manner.
    - Example: Updated datasets distributed on IPFS will include new CIDs reflecting changes, while SQL datasets will be accessible via a queryable interface or downloadable snapshots.

Indexed in a Catalogue

    - Relevance: Metadata for both SQL-based and IPFS-hosted secondary datasets will be indexed in Zenodo or other FAIR repositories, ensuring discoverability.
    Example: Metadata includes dataset descriptions, SQL schemas, update frequencies, and links to IPFS or SQL access points.

Direct Handling of Data Requests

    - Relevance: For users requiring access to SQL databases or large dynamic datasets not easily accessible via IPFS, requests will be mediated by the consortium.
    - Example: This ensures compliance with licensing terms and proper attribution while providing tailored access for academic, non-profit, or commercial use.

textListValues.textListValue: Deposit in a trustworthy data repository

field.textValue:
Primary Identifiers:
        Blockchain Hashes:
            Used for primary onchain data.
            Guarantees immutability and persistence through the decentralized nature of blockchain networks.
        IPFS Content Identifiers (CIDs):
            Used for primary and secondary datasets stored on IPFS.
            Ensures data integrity, persistence, and accessibility across distributed networks.

    Secondary Identifiers:
        DOIs (Digital Object Identifiers):
            Assigned to metadata records stored in Zenodo or other FAIR-compliant repositories.
            Provides a globally recognized and citable reference for datasets, linking to their IPFS CIDs or blockchain hashes.

Advantages of This Approach

    Resilience: Blockchain hashes and IPFS CIDs are inherently decentralized, ensuring that datasets remain accessible even if centralized repositories face limitations.
    Immutability: Both blockchain hashes and IPFS CIDs guarantee that referenced data has not been altered.
    Interoperability: DOIs provide academic and institutional users with a familiar, widely used system for citing and reusing data.
    Scalability: The combination of blockchain, IPFS, and DOIs ensures that both large and small datasets are efficiently managed and referenced.

Primary Data:
        - IPR: The raw primary data collected directly from public blockchains has no copyright, as it is publicly available and inherently open.
        - Transformation Impact: However, the act of transforming and organizing this data into a usable format (e.g., structured datasets, curated views) may create a layer of intellectual property. This transformation adds value through processes such as formatting, cleaning, or metadata enrichment.
        - Management:
            The transformed primary data will be governed by the project’s licensing terms, balancing openness with proper attribution for the work done.

    Secondary Data (Derived or Curated Datasets):
        - IPR: Copyright applies to secondary datasets, as these involve significant intellectual effort in cleaning, processing, and enriching the data.
        - Management:
            Secondary datasets are distributed under a Creative Commons BY-NC-SA license for non-commercial use.
            - Commercial use requires a separate paid licensing agreement, managed by the Steering Committee (SC).

    Code and Documentation:
        - IPR: All project code and documentation are protected under copyright and distributed under an open-source license (e.g., MIT or GPL).
        - Management:
            Open-source licenses encourage reuse and reproducibility while requiring attribution to the FairOnChain project.

    Commercial Exploitation:
        - IPR Enforcement: Commercial use of transformed primary data and secondary datasets is strictly governed by licensing agreements. Unauthorized redistribution or use may result in penalties or access revocation.
        - Revenues: Funds generated from commercial licenses are reinvested into maintaining and improving the infrastructure.

    Oversight and Governance:
        The Steering Committee oversees all IPR matters, ensuring compliance with copyright rules and proper attribution for transformed primary and secondary data.
no personal data will be present. So no special access is needed.

Dublin Core

    Priority: High
    Why?
      Dublin Core is a widely accepted standard for metadata in research and open data projects. It provides a simple and interoperable framework to describe datasets with key elements like title, creator, description, and keywords.
    Examples of fields:
        Title, Creator, Subject, Description, Date, Format, Rights.
    Use in the project: It will be the primary standard for general dataset description to ensure accessibility and compliance with FAIR principles.

Schema.org

    Priority: High
    Why?
      Schema.org is a web-focused metadata standard, widely used for publishing structured data online. It enhances discoverability and interoperability with search engines and other platforms.
    Examples of fields:
        Dataset, Keywords, Publisher, Citation, License.
    Use in the project: It will complement Dublin Core to make datasets easily discoverable and accessible via web interfaces and APIs.

PROV-O (W3C Provenance Ontology)

    Priority: Medium
    Why?
      PROV-O is essential for documenting data provenance, especially for tracing the transformations, processes, and origins of data. This is critical for ensuring transparency and reproducibility in blockchain data.
    Examples of fields:
        Entity, Activity, Agent.
    Use in the project: It will be used to record the lifecycle of primary and secondary datasets, including the tools and methods applied during processing.

DCAT (Data Catalog Vocabulary)

    Priority: Medium to Low
    Why?
      DCAT is designed for cataloging datasets and facilitating their discovery through structured catalogs. It is highly relevant if a centralized catalog or portal for datasets is implemented.
    Examples of fields:
        Dataset, Distribution, AccessURL.
    Use in the project: Considered for cataloging datasets if a repository or centralized access system is developed.

1. Alignment with Institutional Policies

    Criteria: Data will be retained if it aligns with institutional policies or research standards requiring long-term preservation of datasets for academic and public use.
    Example: FAIR principles-compliant metadata and datasets relevant to ongoing research projects will be prioritized.

2. Legal and Contractual Obligations

    Criteria: Data will be retained if required under legal or contractual agreements, such as licensing terms for third-party datasets (e.g., CC BY 4.0) or open-source contributions (e.g., GPL or MIT licenses).
    Example: Labeled datasets derived from public blockchain data and subject to licensing terms will be retained for at least the duration specified in agreements.

3. Accessibility of Source Data

    Criteria: Data will be retained if the original source (e.g., blockchain nodes or third-party datasets) is difficult to access or at risk of becoming unavailable.
    Example: Derived secondary datasets will be preserved to ensure accessibility, even if the primary blockchain data is still available elsewhere.

4. Long-Term Value for Research and Society

    Criteria: Data will be prioritized for retention if it has significant value for reproducibility, historical analysis, or future academic, policy, or commercial applications.
    Example: Aggregated blockchain analytics and SQL-based datasets that contribute to financial transparency will be retained.

5. Technical Feasibility and Sustainability

    Criteria: Data will be retained if its storage and preservation are technically feasible and cost-efficient over time.
    Example: Large datasets distributed via IPFS will leverage decentralized infrastructure for long-term preservation without centralized storage costs.

6. Periodic Review

    Criteria: Data will be periodically reviewed every 5 years to evaluate continued relevance, demand, and feasibility of preservation.
    Example: Low-demand datasets may be archived or flagged for potential disposal if deemed redundant.

To ensure consistency, traceability, and efficient distribution of data, the FairOnChain project will implement the following organizational practices:

Primary Data Storage and Distribution
        IPFS for Decentralized Distribution:
        All primary data will be stored on IPFS to enable decentralized and efficient distribution.
            Files will be uploaded to IPFS and assigned unique content identifiers (CIDs) to ensure immutability and verifiability.
            Each CID will be associated with metadata describing the data (e.g., blockchain source, date range, and block range).
        Naming Conventions for Primary Data Files:
        File names will follow a consistent convention for clarity:
            BlockchainName_type_BlockRange.csv (e.g., 2026/9/12/Ethereum_transactions_10000_to_11000.csv).
        Example folder structure for local preparation before IPFS upload:

    /FairOnChain
        /PrimaryData
            /Bitcoin
            /Ethereum
                  /unified
                  /raw
                  /prices
            /Gnosis

Secondary Data Organization

    Folder Structure:
    Secondary data (e.g., aggregated or curated datasets) will follow a hierarchical organization:

    /FairOnChain
        /SecondaryData
            /Aggregated
            /Curated

        Each dataset will include a "ReadMe.md" file detailing the content, processing methods, and metadata.

Version Control

    For primary data:
        Versioning will rely on IPFS CIDs, as the immutability of IPFS ensures that any change results in a new CID.
    For secondary data:
        Files and datasets will follow a versioning system (v1.0, v1.1, etc.), with changes logged in a dedicated changelog file.
        Git repositories will be used for managing scripts and metadata related to secondary data.

Metadata and Provenance

    Each dataset uploaded to IPFS will be accompanied by a metadata file (e.g., Metadata.json), documenting:
        Blockchain source (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum).
        Data collection details (e.g., date range, block range).
        CID reference for cross-linking datasets.
    Metadata standards such as Dublin Core and PROV-O will be used.

Quality Assurance

    Validation scripts will ensure primary data integrity before upload to IPFS.
    Checksums will be generated and stored to verify data accuracy during retrieval.

To ensure the data can be effectively reused by other researchers, the following documentation will be provided:

    Methodology Documentation
        A detailed explanation of the methodologies used to collect and process the data will be included.
            For primary data: A description of how blockchain nodes were set up, data extraction processes (e.g., use of APIs, block parsing scripts), and any filtering applied during collection.
            For secondary data: Information on cleaning, aggregation, and transformation processes, including the tools and software used.
        Documentation will be available as markdown files (Methodology.md) and included in each dataset folder.

    Variable Definitions and Metadata
        Comprehensive definitions of all variables in the datasets, including:
            Field names, formats, and units of measurement (e.g., timestamps in UTC, amounts in satoshis/ETH).
            For secondary data: Derived metrics (e.g., transaction volume per day, clustering coefficients) will be clearly defined.
        This information will be integrated into metadata files (Metadata.json) and described in ReadMe.md files.

    Analytical and Procedural Information
        Details of the tools, libraries, and frameworks used during data analysis and processing, including:
            Software versions (e.g., Python 3.9, Pandas library v1.4.0).
            ETL pipeline descriptions, with flow diagrams if necessary.
        Example scripts (stored in the /Scripts folder) will be provided to demonstrate data retrieval and processing workflows.

    Provenance Tracking
        Using PROV-O metadata standards, all datasets will include details about the origin and transformations applied to the data.
            Example: "Data collected from Ethereum node using Geth v1.10 on 2024-01-15 and filtered for transactions exceeding 1 ETH."

    Units of Measurement
        Units for all numeric values will be clearly specified:
            For primary data: Blockchain-native units (e.g., satoshis for Bitcoin, gwei for Ethereum).
            For secondary data: Standardized units (e.g., USD for monetary values).

    Access Instructions
        A user guide will describe:
            How to retrieve data from IPFS using CIDs.
            How to query SQL databases or access data via APIs.

    License and Citation Information
        Documentation will clearly state usage rights and licensing (e.g., Creative Commons or custom licenses).
        Citation formats will be provided to encourage proper acknowledgment of the datasets.

    Example Use Cases
        Example analyses or workflows (e.g., Jupyter notebooks) will be included to demonstrate potential re-use scenarios.

textListValues.textListValue: Legal issues such as IP rights
Security issues for organisations or countries

field.textValue:
Ethical Issues to Consider

    Legal Issues such as IP Rights
        Consideration:
        Intellectual property rights apply to third-party datasets and any derived data. For example:
            MBAL (CC0: Public Domain): No restrictions, but acknowledgment is encouraged as best practice.
            Aggregated Data for Blockchain Subsystems (CC BY 4.0): Requires proper attribution for reuse and redistribution.
            Derived datasets must comply with original licensing terms to avoid misuse.
        Action:
            Adhere to licensing requirements for third-party datasets.
            Ensure redistribution complies with the governance framework and original licenses.
            Properly attribute all data sources where required.

    Commercial Interests or Patents
        Consideration:
        The project involves commercial access to datasets. Balancing fair access for researchers with the financial sustainability of the infrastructure is critical.
        Action:
            Commercial users are required to license data under tiered agreements to offset infrastructure costs.
            Non-commercial users (e.g., academic and non-profit users) are prioritized to ensure fairness and open access.

    Security Issues for Organisations or Countries
        Consideration:
        The infrastructure must be secure against unauthorized access or misuse, particularly for critical project files like code and documentation.
        Action:
            Ensure secure data access protocols (e.g., IPFS redundancy, role-based access control).
            Protect code and non-reconstructible documentation through version-controlled systems and automated backups.

Additional Considerations

    - No Personal Data Involved:
        The datasets (primary and secondary) are derived from public blockchain data and do not contain personally identifiable information (PII).
        Privacy concerns are therefore not applicable.

    - Adherence to Ethical Guidelines:
        The project complies with international standards such as the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) for data management.
        Governance by the Steering Committee ensures all ethical concerns, particularly around data licensing and commercial access, are addressed.

MBAL: 10M Crypto Address Label Dataset

    Source: Kaggle - MBAL Dataset. (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/yidongchaintoolai/mbal-10m-crypto-address-label-dataset)
    Description: A dataset containing labeled information for over 10 million cryptocurrency addresses. The labels classify addresses into categories such as exchanges, DeFi protocols, smart contracts, and more.
    Purpose: Provides labeled data to enrich onchain transaction analyses, enabling identification of key participants and activities in the blockchain ecosystem.

Aggregated Data for Blockchain Subsystems

    Source: Dataset from the last screenshot (title: "Aggregated Data for Blockchain Subsystems"). (https://figshare.com/articles/dataset/Aggregated_Data_for_Blockchain_Subsystems/24588711?file=43198467)
    Description: A comprehensive dataset containing aggregated metrics for various blockchain subsystems, including transaction patterns, protocol-level activity, and smart contract interactions.
    Purpose: Enables comparative analyses across blockchain protocols and supports deeper insights into DeFi and NFT ecosystems.

Onchain Price Data from Oracles

    Source: Blockchain-based oracle services such as Chainlink or Tellor, with price feeds directly recorded on Ethereum or other blockchains.
    Description: Decentralized price data for cryptocurrencies, sourced directly from onchain oracle updates.
    Purpose: Provides reliable and tamper-proof price data for analyzing token movements and DeFi protocol interactions.

DEX Transaction Data

    Source: Decentralized exchanges (DEXs) such as Uniswap, Curve, or SushiSwap, with data extracted from onchain logs of smart contract interactions.
    Description: Includes transaction details like token swaps, liquidity provisioning, and pool updates, directly from smart contract event logs.
    Purpose: Enables detailed studies of market behavior, liquidity trends, and price formation mechanisms on DEXs.

___
Provenance and Documentation

    Source Attribution:
        Each dataset will be documented with its source, including API endpoints for oracles and smart contract addresses for DEX logs.
        For the MBAL dataset and the aggregated data from the screenshot, details will include filenames, retrieval dates, and licensing terms.
    Licensing:
        Datasets from open platforms (e.g., Kaggle) and publicly available blockchain data will be redistributed in accordance with their open-data licenses.
    Metadata:
        Each dataset will include metadata files describing its origin, processing methods, and intended use cases.

CSV and JSON (Primary data):

  • CSV: This is a widely supported format across disciplines because it is commonly used for tabular data storage and exchange.
  • JSON: While not always formally standardized in some disciplines, it is a de facto standard in my discipline for structured and hierarchical data, especially in blockchain and API contexts.
SQL and Parquet (Secondary data):

  • SQL: Typically a de facto standard in my discipline, as it is widely adopted for relational databases in research and industry.
  • Parquet: Fits widely supported format across disciplines due to its adoption in Big Data ecosystems and data warehouses.

Primary Data:
        Preservation Strategy:
        Primary data, derived directly from public blockchains, benefits from inherent long-term preservation and high resilience due to the decentralized nature of blockchains. The data is continuously duplicated and maintained across thousands of nodes worldwide, ensuring its availability as long as the blockchain remains active and in use.
        Additionally, this data will be distributed via IPFS, further enhancing redundancy and accessibility.
        Retention Period:
            Blockchain data will remain preserved for the lifetime of the respective blockchain, which is ensured by its active use and community maintenance.
            IPFS copies will also be retained indefinitely, supported by consortium members and user-driven storage.

    Secondary Data:
        Preservation Strategy:
            SQL-based secondary datasets will be preserved in two forms:
                SQL Database Backups: Stored in secure, redundant systems.
                Dynamic Datasets on IPFS: These datasets will be distributed with versioning to maintain both current and historical records.
            Secondary datasets derived from blockchain data can also be reconstructed from primary data if needed.
        Retention Period:
            Secondary datasets will be retained for at least 10 years to support research reproducibility and long-term access.
            After 10 years, their relevance will be reviewed periodically, and a decision will be made to either continue preservation or archive the data.

    Critical Data (Code and Documentation):
        Preservation Strategy:
            Code and documentation essential for data processing and analysis will be preserved in version-controlled repositories (e.g., GitHub, GitLab).
            Backups of these repositories will be stored in redundant systems to ensure protection against loss.
        Retention Period:
            Retained indefinitely to ensure reproducibility and to support future research and project evolution.

Key Considerations:

    - Blockchain Data Resilience:
        The decentralized and distributed architecture of blockchains naturally ensures data preservation for as long as the blockchain is active.
        This resilience reduces the need for additional backup mechanisms for primary data.
    - IPFS for Supplementary Redundancy:
        IPFS will complement blockchain preservation by offering decentralized access to additional dataset copies, ensuring availability even if blockchain nodes become temporarily unavailable.

Governance and Review:

    Periodic Review:
        - The Steering Committee will review the relevance and accessibility of secondary and IPFS-hosted datasets every 5 years to ensure efficient resource use.
    Data Migration:
        - If new technologies emerge, data may be migrated to updated storage formats or systems to ensure long-term usability and accessibility.

The timeline for making the data available follows the structured phases outlined in the project proposal, adjusted to reflect a revised schedule:

    Primary Data Availability:
        Start Date: Beginning in Q4 2024, raw primary data extracted from blockchain nodes will be shared as part of the first major milestone.
        Continuous Updates: Once available, the primary data will be distributed continuously via IPFS and updated regularly to reflect the latest blockchain activity.
        Reason for Timeline: This ensures sufficient time to validate the data extraction pipelines and establish the IPFS distribution network.

    Secondary Data Availability:
        Initial Release: The first version of curated and derived datasets (e.g., labeled and structured data in SQL or IPFS format) will be made available by Q2 2025, following the completion of the data processing workflows.
        Regular Updates: These datasets will be updated at regular intervals (e.g., monthly or quarterly) based on new data derived from primary sources.
        Reason for Timeline: Derived datasets require additional processing, validation, and enrichment, which will be developed during the first two years of the project.

    Critical Data (Code and Documentation):
        Release Timeline:
            Preliminary versions of the code (e.g., data extraction scripts, processing pipelines) and documentation will be published in Q1 2025 to ensure early transparency and reproducibility.
            Finalized and comprehensive versions will be released by Q2 2025 alongside the first curated datasets.
        Ongoing Updates: These resources will be continuously updated and version-controlled throughout the project’s duration.

Commercial Licensing:
        Certain secondary datasets and enriched datasets (e.g., labeled SQL data or aggregated blockchain metrics) are subject to commercial licensing agreements. This ensures that infrastructure costs for the project are offset and sustainability is maintained.
        Commercial users must enter into a data-sharing agreement to access these datasets under specific terms.

    Resource Management:
        To prevent overuse of resources (e.g., API calls, bulk downloads), access to high-demand datasets may be mediated. Academic and non-profit users will receive priority access, while commercial users may have usage quotas.

    Ethical and Legal Compliance:
        Licensing terms for third-party datasets (e.g., CC BY 4.0) and governance rules for derived data require adherence to redistribution and attribution standards.
        Mediated access ensures proper acknowledgment of data sources and compliance with legal and ethical standards.

Actions to Minimise Restrictions

    Public Access to Primary Data:
        All primary data extracted from blockchain nodes will remain publicly accessible via IPFS. This ensures a large portion of the data is freely available to all communities without restrictions.

    Mediated Access for Secondary Data:
        A tiered access system will prioritize academic and non-profit users for free or subsidized access. Commercial users will have paid access based on their usage needs.
        Data-sharing agreements will be streamlined to minimize administrative burden, with clear terms outlined for different user roles.

    Transparency Through Metadata:
        Metadata for all datasets (primary and secondary) will be publicly available in FAIR-enabling repositories (e.g., Zenodo). This ensures discoverability even if certain datasets require mediated access.

    Infrastructure Optimization:
        Dynamic datasets distributed via IPFS reduce dependency on centralized systems, allowing open access while mitigating the risk of excessive resource consumption.

Specific Measures

    Automated Access Control:
        A secure system will manage role-based access to restricted datasets. Users will register as academic, non-profit, or commercial and be granted access based on their role and licensing agreements.

    Collaboration Incentives:
        Researchers and non-profit organizations can gain priority access to secondary datasets by collaborating with the project or contributing resources (e.g., node operation or IPFS support).

    Review of Access Policies:
        The Steering Committee will periodically review restrictions to adapt them to evolving community needs and technological capabilities.

MBAL: 10M Crypto Address Label Dataset (Kaggle):
            License: CC0: Public Domain.
            Restrictions: No restrictions on reuse or redistribution. The dataset is openly available for any purpose, including commercial use, without requiring attribution.

        Aggregated Data for Blockchain Subsystems:
            License: CC BY 4.0 (Attribution).
            Restrictions: Reuse, modification, and redistribution are permitted, including for commercial purposes, as long as proper attribution is provided to the original source.

        Onchain Price Data from Oracles (e.g., Chainlink, Tellor):
            License: Onchain data is inherently public and freely available as part of the blockchain ecosystem.
            Restrictions: While oracle data is open for analysis and use, redistribution or large-scale aggregation might be subject to terms defined by governance frameworks or oracle providers.

        DEX Transaction Data (e.g., Uniswap, Curve, SushiSwap):
            License: Onchain transaction logs are public as part of the blockchain ledger.
            Restrictions: No explicit restrictions for analysis, but redistribution of processed datasets may require acknowledgment of the DEX ecosystem contributors.

License Values to List:

    CC0: Public Domain (MBAL).
    CC BY 4.0 (Aggregated Data for Blockchain Subsystems).
    Open/Public License (for onchain data such as DEX transactions and oracles).

Governance and Compliance:

    MBAL Dataset: No restrictions apply as it is licensed under CC0. It can be freely reused and redistributed for any purpose.
    Aggregated Data for Blockchain Subsystems: Reuse and redistribution must include proper attribution to comply with the CC BY 4.0 license.
    Other onchain data, such as from oracles and DEX transactions, will be governed by their respective public or open licenses.
    The Steering Committee (SC) will oversee compliance with licensing terms to ensure proper attribution and avoid misuse of data.

Primary Data:
      Raw blockchain transaction data:
  •  Estimated volume: ~0.5–1 TB per year per blockchain (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum).
  •  For three blockchains (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Gnosis, Lukso, Hoodi & Holesky): ~1.5–3 TB per year.
  • Unified format in CSV 
  •  Data format: CSV (compressed for storage efficiency) and JSON for specific hierarchical structures.
Secondary Data:
       Aggregated datasets (curated views, enriched metadata):
  • Estimated volume: ~100–300 GB per year, depending on the number of curated datasets.
  • Storage efficiency may increase with the use of Parquet for larger datasets.
  • Data format: SQL-based storage for structured data, with exports to CSV or Parquet.
  • OpenPrice data from Uniswap and chainlink for eth and ERC20 at the end
Summary of Total Volumes

    Total estimated volume: ~2–4 TB per year (primary + secondary data).
    Number of files:
  • Primary data: Millions of records (rows) per blockchain, segmented by date (day) or block.
  • Secondary data: Hundreds of curated datasets, each containing thousands to millions of rows.

Veton Berisha
Christian Russo
Stéphane Augusto

Amir alwash
